articles.title: Worth Nothing — and That Changes Everything
articles.dek: Paul stood at Miletus, told the people he loved most that prison was waiting ahead of him, then walked toward the ship anyway. His reason reshapes the way we hold our own lives.
articles.quote: I consider my life worth nothing to me; my only aim is to finish the race and complete the task the Lord Jesus has given me—the task of testifying to the gospel of God's grace.
articles.body:
The queue at NAIA Terminal 1 moves slowly. A father stands near the departure gate, his son beside him with a roller bag and a signed contract for two years in Qatar. The father knows the math: two years is 730 days without this face at the dinner table. He does not cry — not in front of his son. But his hands, when he grips his boy's shoulders one last time, say everything his voice cannot.

Filipinos know this scene by heart. Millions have lived it. And it is precisely this kind of moment that unlocks what is happening in one of the most quietly devastating passages in the entire New Testament.

Paul is in Miletus, a port city on the western coast of what is now Turkey. He needs to reach Jerusalem before Pentecost, so he cannot afford the detour to Ephesus — about 50 kilometers north. Instead, he sends word to the elders of the church there: Come down to me. They come. And when they arrive, Paul sits them down and begins to tell them what he has been carrying.

He starts by looking back. "You know how I lived the whole time I was with you, from the first day I came into the province of Asia. I served the Lord with great humility and with tears and in the midst of severe testing by the plots of my Jewish opponents." (Acts 20:18–19, NIV) This is not a polished ministry report. This is a man being honest about how hard it was. The tears. The plots. The cost of years poured out for people he loved.

Then he looks forward — and the room goes quiet.

"And now, compelled by the Spirit, I am going to Jerusalem, not knowing what will happen to me there. I only know that in every city the Holy Spirit warns me that prison and hardships are waiting for me." (Acts 20:22–23, NIV)

Read that again. In every city, the same warning. Prison. Hardships. And still he is going. Not because he is reckless, not because he has misread what the Spirit is telling him. He understands perfectly. He is choosing to go anyway. Then he says the line that defines why:

"I consider my life worth nothing to me; my only aim is to finish the race and complete the task the Lord Jesus has given me — the task of testifying to the gospel of God's grace." (Acts 20:24, NIV)

Notice the phrase carefully: worth nothing to me. Not worthless in God's eyes. Not worthless to the people in this room who are about to weep over him. Worth nothing to Paul himself — meaning his own life holds no veto power over the direction God has given him. If it costs him everything, the race still runs. The task still matters more than the man.

Here is the fresh perspective worth sitting with: this speech at Miletus is the only farewell address in the New Testament where Paul speaks not to strangers, not to opponents, not to a crowd — but to people he loves, people who love him back, people who built something together over years of shared labor and shared tears. These elders wept with him during the hard seasons. They prayed together in homes and marketplaces. And now he is telling them: I may never see your face again, and I am still going.

That is a different kind of courage. Not the adrenaline courage of a man who does not know what is coming. The settled courage of a man who knows exactly what is coming and has already made peace with it.

There is one more detail in this passage worth pausing on. In verse 35, Paul quotes Jesus: "It is more blessed to give than to receive." Search Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — you will not find these words anywhere. This is the only saying of Jesus recorded in the Bible that does not appear in any of the four Gospels. Paul carried it — likely passed down from those who walked with Jesus personally — and he lived it out loud. The man who worked with his own hands making tents so he would never be a financial burden to anyone had learned something true: giving costs something real, and the cost is the point. What you spend yourself on is what you actually believe in.

Paul's life was that offering.

Now the question turns toward you and me. Most of us are not being called to Jerusalem with prison waiting at the end of the road. But we each have a race. And we each have things that quietly hold veto power over the direction God is nudging us.

Sometimes it is comfort — a habit that has become so familiar it has quietly become a chain. Sometimes it is reputation, the reasonable-sounding fear of what the barkada will say, or what the family will think, if we actually follow through on what we feel called to. Sometimes it is the voice of safety: Wait until the timing is better. Wait until you have more resources. Wait until the risk feels smaller. Paul had every reason to wait. The Spirit was literally warning him city by city. He went anyway.

The Filipino virtue of malasakit — that fierce, bone-deep care for the people around us — is one of the most beautiful things about our culture. But sometimes we let it run in reverse. Instead of giving sacrificially, we use the people we love as the reason we cannot move. I cannot take the risk — they need me stable. I cannot say yes to this calling — what about my family? Paul's departure did not protect his loved ones from grief. It broke their hearts. But it was still the right choice. And he did not slip away quietly — he knelt with them and prayed, face to face, holding nothing back.

After Paul finished speaking, he knelt down with all of them and prayed. "They all wept as they embraced him and kissed him. What grieved them most was his saying that they would never see his face again. And they accompanied him to the ship." (Acts 20:37–38, NIV)

No triumphant music. No slow-motion send-off. Just grown people weeping on a dock, watching a man they love walk toward suffering because he has decided that finishing the race is worth more than finishing comfortable.

Today, ask yourself honestly: What is one thing I am holding right now that holds veto power over what God is asking me to do? Name it. Then bring it — like Paul brought his whole life — to a moment of open-handed prayer. You do not need to see the full road ahead. Paul did not. He only knew the direction and the One who called him.

The race is not finished yet. Finish it well.

articles.title: Put Down Your Poles
articles.dek: For hundreds of years, the Levites' calling was to carry the sacred tent on their shoulders. Then David, in one of his last acts as king, told them to put it down.
articles.quote: The carrying was honorable — but the praise was always the point.
articles.body:
Every January in Manila, the streets of Quiapo go still then wild. Hundreds of thousands of devotees crowd the procession of the Black Nazarene, and among them are the hijos — the volunteers who take turns shouldering the massive camarín, the gilded carriage that holds the dark-wooden image. Their necks bow. Their shoulders strain. They do not look left or right. To carry the sacred thing is considered a privilege. But anyone who has done it will tell you: it is also an enormous, breath-shortening weight.

For centuries, one entire tribe of Israel knew exactly what that felt like. Not once a year, but every time the nation moved.

Before Israel had a temple — before God's presence had a permanent address — the Israelites worshiped on the move. The tabernacle was a sacred, portable tent: its curtains rolled up, its frames unbolted, its poles slid through rings, its altars wrapped in cloth. And it was the Levites' appointed job to carry all of it. Numbers 4 describes the task with meticulous care: each clan had specific items to transport, specific coverings to use. The minimum age was thirty — presumably because this labor required men in their physical prime. They were carriers. Sacred carriers. But carriers.

By the time we reach 1 Chronicles 23, the world has changed. David is old — the text says he is “full of years” (v. 1) — and he has gathered all the leaders of Israel to make Solomon king. But before he steps back, he does something nobody asked for: he reorganizes the Levites from the ground up. He counts them: 38,000 men thirty years and older. Then he parcels out their assignments. Twenty-four thousand to supervise the future temple's work. Six thousand as officials and judges. Four thousand as gatekeepers. And then — this is the part worth slowing down for — four thousand men assigned to do nothing but praise the Lord with musical instruments David himself had designed (1 Chr 23:5).

Then David lowers the minimum age from thirty to twenty. Why? He explains it himself, and it is one of the most quietly revolutionary sentences in the Levitical records:

“Since the LORD, the God of Israel, has granted rest to his people and has come to dwell in Jerusalem forever, the Levites no longer need to carry the tabernacle or any of the articles used in its service.” — 1 Chronicles 23:25–26

Read that twice. The reason the age changes is not because they found younger, stronger men. The reason is that God has settled. Israel is at rest. A permanent temple is coming. And because of what God has done — the carrying is over. You no longer need to carry.

Here is what is easy to miss. David did not just reduce their load — he replaced it with something far better. “They were also to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD. They were to do the same in the evening” (1 Chr 23:30). Morning. Evening. Every day. Praise. The four thousand musicians received instruments. The thousands more received new roles around the temple. The Levites went from being the nation's sacred moving crew to being Israel's full-time worshipers. The carrying was honorable — but the praise was always the point.

Think about that transition for a moment. For generations, these men's identity was built around strain and sacrifice. Physical work. Faithful work. Necessary work. And then an old king, near the end of his life, gathers them and says: That season is done. Put down your poles. Pick up your instruments. Stand and sing.

It is worth asking: are you still carrying something God has already settled?

This is not a small question. Many of us were formed in a carrying season. Maybe you carried your family through a long financial stretch — those remittance years, the OFW sacrifices, sending money home before keeping a peso for yourself. Maybe you carried a broken relationship, hauling the weight of what was said or done years ago. Maybe you carried a private shame no one else could see. You carried faithfully. Perhaps even honorably. Because the season demanded it. But seasons end. And sometimes the announcement does not come with a fanfare. Sometimes it comes quietly, in an old man's reorganization of a list — 38,000 names, new assignments, and one sentence: They no longer need to carry.

One of the quiet gifts of the Christian life is that God does not simply add rest on top of our burdens — He sometimes restructures the whole assignment. Guilt you have carried for years may already be forgiven, fully and completely, at the cross. Anxiety about a future you cannot control may belong to the God who already holds it. The weight of performance — the feeling that you must prove your worth, earn your welcome, justify your existence — that too is a load from an older season. Jesus already settled it. The temple has been built. You do not need to carry the tabernacle anymore.

The Levites could have resisted this. They could have said, “But carrying is what we do. It is who we are.” We do not know that they did. What we know is that David gave the announcement and the assignments changed. Sometimes the most faithful thing is to simply believe the announcement — to stop waiting for the poles to feel lighter and just put them down.

We have a word for this in Filipino: pahinga. Not laziness — Filipinos are not lazy people. But rest. The exhale after hard labor. The mano you give your nanay when you finally get home. The moment you take off your shoes at the door and just sit. David looked at thousands of men shaped by decades of hauling and said, in effect: Pahinga na. You have carried enough. Now come and praise.

This Sunday, before you pick up your familiar load — the anxiety, the shame, the low-grade dread, the pressure to perform — pause just long enough to ask: Is this still mine to carry? Or has God already settled this?

And if the answer is the latter, do what the Levites did. Stand up. Morning and evening. And praise. You are not just a porter. The temple is being built. The poles can go down. Pick up your instrument. God is listening.

articles.title: The Stories Are Not Yours to Keep
articles.dek: Psalm 78 opens with a simple command — and it turns out the most powerful defense against spiritual drift is a grandparent who talks.
articles.quote: The stories are not yours to keep.
articles.body:
Picture an ordinary Philippine evening — maybe a Saturday, the kind where the heat finally breaks around seven and someone opens the windows. The electric fan hums. The television stays off for once. Lola is sitting in the sala, and three grandchildren are sprawled on the tiled floor, half-watching her, half-playing on their phones. Nobody planned anything. But the youngest one — maybe eight years old, curious in the way that eight-year-olds still are — looked up and asked: "Lola, how did you become a Christian?"

Two hours later, dinner is cold. Nobody has moved. Nobody minded.

That moment — unplanned, unhurried, irreplaceable — is precisely what Psalm 78 was written to protect.

Psalm 78 is one of the longest psalms in the entire Bible, seventy-two verses of compressed Israelite history. But it begins quietly, with a musician clearing his throat. His name is Asaph, a Levite appointed by King David to lead worship. He is not a king. He is not a prophet. He is a poet and a singer who has been watching something happen to his people, and he is deeply concerned.

"My people, hear my teaching; listen to the words of my mouth," he begins (Psalm 78:1, NIV). Then he says something striking: he will speak in parables, in "hidden things, things from of old" (Psalm 78:2, NIV). These are not secrets God wants buried. They are wonders God wants remembered. The Hebrew word translated "hidden things" carries the sense of riddles — fascinating things that beg to be examined closely, and then passed on.

And so Asaph does what every Filipino lola does instinctively: he reaches back into the past and starts talking.

What he reaches back to matters. Not military victories. Not the genealogies of impressive men. He wants to tell the next generation about God himself — "the praiseworthy deeds of the Lord, his power, and the wonders he has done" (Psalm 78:4, NIV). He wants them to know what God did. He wants them to feel the weight of it.

This was not Asaph's invention. God himself had commanded the ancestors to teach their children, so those children would teach their children, so the generation not yet born would already know the God who acts in history (Psalm 78:5–6). Faith was designed to travel — generation to generation, like a lamp passed down a long corridor, each holder responsible to keep it lit before handing it forward.

Then Asaph names the one thing that can break the chain: forgetting.

"Then they would put their trust in God and would not forget his deeds but would keep his commands." — Psalm 78:7 (NIV)

Notice what Asaph binds together in that single verse: trust, memory, and obedience. They are not three separate virtues. They are one chain. You trust God when you remember what he has done. You obey God when you trust him. Let the middle link — memory — go slack, and the whole thing fails.

This is worth sitting with, because we rarely think of telling stories as a spiritual discipline. Quiet time, prayer, fasting — these feel like faith. Gathering the grandchildren and saying, "Let me tell you what God did for our family" — that can feel like mere reminiscing. But Asaph is insisting they are the same thing. The storytelling is the discipleship. The remembering is the resistance.

Look at verse 8. When Asaph imagines the generation that stopped hearing the stories, he does not say they became skeptics in a flash. He says they became "a stubborn and rebellious generation, whose hearts were not loyal to God, whose spirits were not faithful to him" (Psalm 78:8, NIV). They did not choose rebellion as a first move. They forgot. And forgetting, it turns out, is not neutral. Left untended, it tilts toward faithlessness the way an untended path tilts toward weeds.

This is Asaph's urgency. He is not an old man lost in nostalgia. He is a watchman. And the alarm he is sounding is quiet but absolutely clear: tell them. Tell them before you forget. Tell them before they drift.

Here is the question Psalm 78 presses into your hands: what story of God's faithfulness are you carrying, and who have you told?

Maybe it is the diagnosis that came back clean. Maybe it is the job offer that arrived the week the savings ran out. Maybe it is the relationship that was this close to breaking — and somehow it held. Maybe it is quieter than that: a peace that settled on you one afternoon in prayer when you were sure it would not come. That is a praiseworthy deed of the Lord. That is exactly the kind of wonder Asaph wants lodged in the hearts of the next generation.

Filipino families are often remarkably good at this, even if we never frame it as theology. Tatay mentions at dinner, almost offhand, the time the typhoon was supposed to hit directly but veered away at the last moment. Nanay talks while chopping vegetables about the month they could not pay tuition and God provided in a way she still cannot fully explain. These are not formal testimonies with three points and a closing prayer. They are just how Filipino believers talk when they are not performing — ordinary speech, salted with God's name.

Asaph would recognize that. He would say: keep doing that. Be intentional. Do not let those stories die with you.

If you have children nearby, or nephews, nieces, or young neighbors — find one moment this week to tell them one thing God did for you. Not a lecture. Not a Bible study. Just: "Let me tell you about a time." That is the whole assignment.

And if you are the young one in the room — ask the question. Ask Lola. Ask the oldest believer you know: "How did you first trust God?" Then listen carefully. What you hear is not just family history. It is the living record of God's faithfulness, waiting to be carried forward.

Psalm 78 begins with the word "hear" and ends its opening movement with a warning about the generation that did not. In between, there is a vision: an unbroken chain, generation to generation, story to story, trust built on memory, obedience flowing from trust.

You are one link in that chain. You did not choose when you entered it, or who first handed the lamp to you. But you do choose whether the chain holds through you.

Tell them what God did. Tell them while you still remember. Tell them in a way they can carry and pass on.

The stories are not yours to keep.

articles.title: She Won't Stop Calling
articles.dek: Proverbs pictures Wisdom shouting in the streets like a market vendor nobody notices. That image should stop us cold — and then comfort us.
articles.quote: Wisdom is not hiding. She never was.
articles.body:
On a busy Saturday morning in Quiapo, before the sun has fully risen, a woman is already arranging her stall. By seven o'clock the market is fully alive — vendors calling out, jeepneys pushing through, the thick smell of incense drifting from the church two streets over and mixing with the sharp scent of fish and fresh ginger laid on the table. The woman sells luya and dilaw, and her voice rides above the noise: Ate! Kuya! Dito na, sariwang-sariwa!

Most people walk past without looking up. Some have earphones in. Some are deep in their phones. A few look directly at her and keep walking anyway. She does not stop calling. She has been doing this for twenty years. She knows that some people will stop, and she calls out for those people — the ones who might actually listen if she is just persistent enough.

Three thousand years ago and half a world away, a poet sat down and described this exact scene. But the voice calling out was not selling ginger. She was offering something far more valuable — and receiving far more indifference.

The book of Proverbs personifies wisdom as a woman, and the first chapter introduces her with a detail that should stop a reader cold. Wisdom is not tucked away in a temple, waiting for the worthy to come find her. She is not whispering to a chosen few behind closed doors. She has gone out into the streets, and she is shouting.

"Out in the open wisdom calls aloud, she raises her voice in the public square; on top of the wall she cries out, at the city gate she makes her speech." (Proverbs 1:20–21, NIV)

In ancient Israel, the city gate was the beating heart of public life. Markets were held there. Contracts were witnessed there. Judges settled disputes at the gate. If you wanted the whole city to hear something — really hear it — you went to the gate and raised your voice. That is precisely where Wisdom plants herself. Not in private. Not among the already-convinced. At the loudest, most trafficked, most distracted place she could find.

Her message, when you strip it down, is not complicated. She asks: how long are you going to keep living this way? How long will the simple love their simplicity, the mocker love his mockery? (Proverbs 1:22) She is not cold when she asks this — she is urgent. There is a difference between anger and urgency. Anger wants to punish. Urgency wants to reach you before it is too late.

Then the passage takes a sharp and uncomfortable turn. Wisdom describes what happens when her calling goes unanswered long enough: "But since you refuse to listen when I call and no one pays attention when I stretch out my hand, since you disregard all my advice and do not accept my rebuke, I in turn will laugh at your disaster; I will mock when calamity overtakes you — when calamity overtakes you like a storm, when disaster sweeps over you like a whirlwind." (Proverbs 1:24–26, NIV)

That sounds severe. But read it again carefully. The calamity she describes — the storm, the whirlwind — is not something Wisdom sends. It is the natural outcome of a life that has ignored wisdom for long enough. She is not the storm. She is the one who warned you about the storm. And in her words, underneath what sounds like mockery, is something that sounds more like grief — the grief of a person who called and called and was simply not heard.

"Out in the open wisdom calls aloud, she raises her voice in the public square." — Proverbs 1:20, NIV

Here is the detail worth sitting with this Sunday: Wisdom chose the noisiest, most distracted place she could find — and she went there on purpose.

She could have stayed somewhere quiet and let the serious ones come find her. She could have made herself available only to the spiritually prepared. But she did not. She went to the marketplace. She went to the city gate. She went where ordinary, busy, distracted people were living their ordinary, busy, distracted lives — and she raised her voice there. This is grace before it had a name.

Most of us picture wisdom as something we must earn our way toward — climb high enough, study hard enough, suffer enough, and maybe wisdom will grant you an audience. Proverbs turns that idea completely over. Wisdom is not passive. Wisdom pursues. Wisdom goes looking for you in the middle of your noise.

When you reach the New Testament, this image becomes a person. In 1 Corinthians 1:24, Paul calls Christ "the wisdom of God." The same Wisdom who called out in the public square is the one who walked those streets in sandals — who stopped at a tax collector's booth, who spoke to a woman at a well in the heat of the afternoon, who ate dinner with people that respectable society had written off entirely. He did not wait for people to clean themselves up and come find him. He went to the gate. He raised his voice. He stretched out his hand.

And Proverbs 1:24 puts it with precision: "no one pays attention when I stretch out my hand." A hand stretched out — in welcome, in help, in invitation — and people walking past it.

We are those people, more often than we would like to admit. Not because we are dramatically rebellious. Most of us are simply busy. The children need breakfast. The group chat is going. There is a deadline on Monday and it is already Sunday and the whole week just disappeared again. Wisdom is calling somewhere in the background and we genuinely mean to get to her soon — just not right now.

The passage does not condemn busyness. It asks us to notice the pattern. When the noise finally clears — when the hard news arrives, when the relationship fractures, when the storm comes in and you are suddenly very small — we wonder where God was. Proverbs gently, firmly suggests that he was at the gate the whole time, calling in the marketplace, and we were the ones who had our heads down.

The invitation today is not complicated. Before the Sunday noise begins — before the family lunch, before the second cup of rice, before the afternoon traffic back home — just stop for five minutes. Sit with the voice that has been calling. You do not need to be impressive to receive what Wisdom is offering. You only need to be present.

And if you have walked past for a long time — maybe years — this passage is not a warning dressed as devotion. It is a portrait of a God who has not grown tired of calling. Wisdom is not hiding. She never was. The storm Wisdom describes is not a punishment she designs; it is the consequence of a road chosen without her. And even now, inside whatever storm you may be standing in, her voice is still carrying. Still calling. Still stretched out in your direction.

She is in this Sunday morning. She is in a passage you almost skipped. She is in the quiet waiting just underneath the noise of your week, if you will stop long enough to hear her. She has been calling your name. And she has not given up.
